file_name: Kablan and Ayas - 2024 - Skin lesion classification from dermoscopy images using ensemble learning of ConvNeXt models.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically an ensemble learning approach combining ConvNeXt models (Tiny, Small, Base, and Large), to classify skin lesions from dermoscopy images using the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' and presents a novel experimental study. It details the design of an ensemble learning approach using ConvNeXt models, describes the methodology (including the ISIC 2019 dataset, training protocols, and metrics), and reports original results such as 97.7% accuracy and 97.9% specificity, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin lesion classification from dermoscopy images using ensemble learning of ConvNeXt models
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion classification from dermoscopy images by proposing a novel ensemble learning approach using ConvNeXt models. The researchers utilized pre-trained and fine-tuned versions of ConvNeXt (Tiny, Small, Base, and Large) on the publicly available ISIC 2019 dataset for eight-class skin lesion classification. Individual model analysis revealed that ConvNeXt-Large achieved the highest accuracy of 97.2% among single architectures. By fusing all four ConvNeXt models using confidence scores, the proposed ensemble method achieved an overall classification accuracy of 97.7%, along with a sensitivity of 84.2% and specificity of 97.9%. These results demonstrate that the ensemble approach surpasses both individual ConvNeXt models and several state-of-the-art methods, providing evidence for its effectiveness in accurately classifying skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Eight-class skin lesion categories (AK, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, SCC, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions from dermoscopy images using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The task involves assigning each test sample to one of eight predefined classes. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics derived from a confusion matrix.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from the 2019 International Skin Imaging Collaboration Grand Challenge, containing dermoscopy images of skin lesions. The dataset was used for multi-class classification of eight skin lesion types.

use.training: The available training data was split into training, validation, and test subsets with a ratio of 70%, 10%, and 20% respectively. Pre-trained ConvNeXt models were fine-tuned on this data using 5-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection during the 5-fold cross-validation process.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the individual ConvNeXt models and the ensemble model using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics.
use.other: The dataset was also used for fair comparison with state-of-the-art methods (Molina et al., Zhao et al., EfficientNets, Kassem et al., CSLNet, Swin Transformer) which utilized the same dataset configuration.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of individual ConvNeXt models and the ensemble model, ensuring that the number of images of the same class is equal in each fold to avoid class representation issues.
To calculate sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics for each class and overall performance by analyzing the relationship between actual and predicted class values.
To address class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset (specifically the over-representation of the NV class) by applying a weighting scheme to the cross-entropy loss function during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (mean accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and standard deviations from 5-fold cross-validation, without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.experiment: 5-fold cross-validation on training data split (70% train, 10% val, 20% test)
ISIC 2019.notes: ConvNeXt-L achieved the highest accuracy (97.2%) among individual models. The ensemble of all four models (T-S-B-L) achieved the best overall performance with 97.7% accuracy, 84.2% sensitivity, and 97.9% specificity. Fold-1 test set specific results for AK class sensitivity: ConvNeXt-T (68%), ConvNeXt-S (76%), ConvNeXt-B (73%), ConvNeXt-L (76%), Ensemble (78%). Fold-1 test set specific results for MEL class accuracy: ConvNeXt-T (74%), Ensemble (78%).

structural_features: Bottleneck structure (feature extraction, dimension reduction, dimension expansion)

training_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy loss with inverse class frequency weighting

classification_or_output_head.details: Individual fine-tuned ConvNeXt classifiers produce confidence scores; final label is selected via arg max of averaged probabilities across the ensemble

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: random horizontal and vertical flips
Data augmentation: color jitter (brightness, contrast, saturation adjustments)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Geometric transformation applied during training to enhance model generalization.

Color transform involving adjustments to brightness, contrast, and saturation.

conclusions: The study is the first to apply ConvNeXt model architectures (Tiny, Small, Base, and Large) to the task of multi-class skin lesion classification from dermoscopy images.
Among individual ConvNeXt models, ConvNeXt-Large achieved the highest accuracy of 97.2% on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
An ensemble approach fusing all four ConvNeXt models using confidence scores achieved an overall classification accuracy of 97.7%, with a sensitivity of 84.2% and specificity of 97.9%.
The proposed ensemble method outperformed both individual ConvNeXt models and several state-of-the-art methods based on CNNs and Vision Transformers.

The findings provide evidence that the proposed ensemble approach effectively and accurately classifies skin lesions, highlighting the potential of ConvNeXt models for automated skin cancer diagnosis systems.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 9,012 NV vs. 168 DF training samples), potentially biasing performance metrics.
No external validation was performed; results are limited to the internal 5-fold cross-validation on the ISIC 2019 dataset, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm the significance of performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods.
Sensitivity for minority classes (DF, SCC, VASC) remains relatively low (e.g., 84.2% overall sensitivity), indicating potential clinical risk for false negatives in rare lesion types.
The ensemble method increases computational complexity and inference time compared to single models, which may impact deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings.
Data leakage risks are mitigated by using 5-fold cross-validation with equal class representation in each fold, but the reliance on a single public dataset limits robustness assessment.
